revision.text:
'''価長'''（かちょう）とは、[[内蔵寮]]（2人）・[[大蔵省]]（4人）・[[東西市司]]（東西各5人）の各官司に設置された[[官人]]（[[伴部]]）。
== 概要 == 都の東西にあった市における物価の調査と売買の監視、官が保有・使用する物資の売買を掌った（「職員令」）。市司では商品の品質に基づいて上中下の等級を設け、更に時価に基づいてその価格を3段階（[[估価]]）に分けて9等の価格を設定するとともに、10日に1度それを記した帳簿（沽価帳）を[[京職]]に提出して官はそのうちの中物・中沽価を購入することとされていた（「関市令」）。沽価帳の作成から、官の物資調達などの実務を行ったのが価長であったと推定されている。
なお、大蔵省の価長は、平安時代初めにおいては実際には2人だけを任じていたのを、[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]2年（[[860年]]）に令本来の規定によって4人を任じることにした。だが、同13年[[2月14日 (旧暦)|2月14日]]（[[871年]][[3月8日]]）の[[太政官符]]によって改めて2人に減員された（『類聚三代格』四）。
== 参考文献 == *多賀宗隼「価長」（『国史大辞典 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） *平岡定海「価長」（『平安時代史事典』（角川書店、1994年） ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かちよう}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

== 概要 == 都の東西にあった市における物価の調査と売買の監視、官が保有・使用する物資の売買を掌った（「職員令」）。市司では商品の品質に基づいて上中下の等級を設け、更に時価に基づいてその価格を3段階（[[估価]]）に分けて9等の価格を設定するとともに、10日に一度それを記した帳簿（沽価帳）を[[京職]]に提出して、官はそのうちの中物・中沽価を購入することとされていた（「関市令」）。沽価帳の作成から、官の物資調達などの実務を行ったのが価長であったと推定されている。
なお、大蔵省の価長は、[[平安時代]]初めにおいては実際には2人だけを任じていたのを、[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]2年（[[860年]]）に令本来の規定によって4人を任じることにした。だが、貞観13年[[2月14日 (旧暦)|2月14日]]（[[871年]][[3月8日]]）の[[太政官符]]によって、改めて2人に減員された（『類聚三代格』四）。
== 参考文献 == * 多賀宗隼「価長」（『国史大辞典 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） * 平岡定海「価長」（『平安時代史事典』（角川書店、1994年） ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かちよう}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market). == Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase..."
revision.text:
'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into upper, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)
[[Category:Ritsuryō system]] [[ja:価長]]

== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into upper, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)

'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into upper, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)

Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into upper, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). '''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into upper, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)

Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into upper, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)

'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into uqqer, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).

'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into u11er, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)

revision.text:
'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into u11er, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)

revision.text:
'''価長'''（かちょう、和語:あきおさ）とは、[[内蔵寮]]（2人）・[[大蔵省 (律令制)|大蔵省]]（4人）・[[東西市司]]（東西各5人）の各官司に設置された[[官人]]（[[伴部]]）。
== 概要 == 都の東西にあった市における物価の調査と売買の監視、官が保有・使用する物資の売買を掌った（「職員令」）。市司では商品の品質に基づいて上中下の等級を設け、更に時価に基づいてその価格を3段階（[[估価]]）に分けて9等の価格を設定するとともに、10日に一度それを記した帳簿（沽価帳）を[[京職]]に提出して、官はそのうちの中物・中沽価を購入することとされていた（「関市令」）。沽価帳の作成から、官の物資調達などの実務を行ったのが価長であったと推定されている。
なお、大蔵省の価長は、[[平安時代]]初めにおいては実際には2人だけを任じていたのを、[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]2年（[[860年]]）に令本来の規定によって4人を任じることにした。だが、貞観13年[[2月14日 (旧暦)|2月14日]]（[[871年]][[3月8日]]）の[[太政官符]]によって、改めて2人に減員された（『類聚三代格』四）。
== 参考文献 == * [[多賀宗隼]]「価長」（『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） * [[平岡定海]]「価長」（『平安時代史事典』（角川書店、1994年） ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かちよう}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:日本の律令制]] into [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into u11er, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[:en:Keishi|Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[:en:Jōgan|Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6) * Hiraoka Jōkai, "Kachō" (*Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia*, [[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7)

Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into u11er, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the [[:en:Keishi|Keishi]] office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the [[:en:Jōgan|Jōgan]] era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).

However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).

'''Kachō''' (価長, Japanese reading: Akiosa) refers to officials ([[官人]]) assigned to various governmental offices during the [[Ritsuryō system]] in ancient Japan, including the [[Naizenshō]] (2 officials), the [[Ōkurashō]] (4 officials), and the [[Tōzai no Ichishi]] (5 officials for each East and West Market).
== Overview == Kachō were responsible for monitoring market prices, overseeing transactions in the East and West Markets, and managing the purchase and sale of goods required or held by the government (*Shokininryō*). Within the market offices (*Ichishi*), products were categorized into u11er, middle, and lower grades based on quality, and their prices were divided into three levels (*Koka*) according to prevailing market values. This resulted in a nine-tiered pricing system. A ledger (*Kokachō*) recording these prices was compiled every 10 days and submitted to the {{ill|Keishi|en|Keishi|WD=Q6383869|lt=Keishi|1=Keishi}} office, with the government mandated to purchase goods of middle quality at middle-level prices (*Kokachō*). Kachō are believed to have been responsible for compiling these ledgers and carrying out practical tasks related to government procurement.
At the [[Ōkurashō]], the number of Kachō was officially increased from 2 to 4 in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in accordance with the original Ritsuryō regulations, as decreed in the second year of the {{ill|Jōgan|en|Jōgan|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} era (860). However, on the 14th day of the second month of the 13th year of Jōgan (March 8, 871), a [[Dajōkanpu]] (imperial decree) reduced the number back to 2 (*Ruijū Sandai Kyaku* Vol. 4).
== References == * Taga Munetoshi, "Kachō" (*{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}*, Vol. 3, [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1983.
